{{Quote|Power is a very peculiar thing. It's like the ego. The ego's only there to keep you above water. Once we realize something good about ourselves, we have a tendency to abuse the gift. That's what this film series is about: truths and consequences. As my wife says, '<nowiki />''Bachi atari''.' That's Japanese for 'What goes around comes around.' I tell my son Corey that the greatest teacher is the teacher who says, 'Don't follow me, follow yourself. Because within you there is that kingdom, that life, that force.' Boy, I guess I sure can't describe my concept of these films any better than that.|Billy Dee Williams, speaking about ''Star Wars'' in 1980|<ref name="Rolling Stone 1980">{{WebCite|url=https://www.rollingstone.com/culture/culture-news/star-wars-slaves-to-the-empire-61931|text='Star Wars': Slaves to the 'Empire'|author=Timothy White|work=[[Wikipedia:Rolling Stone|''Rolling Stone'']]|archivedate=20190519150233}}</ref>}}
'''William December Williams''', better known as '''Billy Dee Williams''' (born [[April 6]], [[1930s#1937|1937]]), is a film, stage, and television actor and painter who played [[Landonis Balthazar Calrissian|Lando Calrissian]] in the [[Star Wars saga|''Star Wars'' saga]]. He starred in [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back'']], [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']], and [[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|''Star Wars'': Episode IX ''The Rise of Skywalker'']].

==Biography==
===Entering ''Empire''===
{{Quote|I talked to George when ''Star Wars'' came out and I was very candid. I said, 'Look, there's Darth Vader, the dark, ''black'' fear, and there's Alec Guinness playing the ''white'' knight, Ben Obi-Wan Kenobi. Here we go again, perpetuating that same old stuff.' And now, by having Lando, we're gonna have to put aside the old point of view. Now, we're talking about ''symbols''—''darkness'' as averse to clarity. Black, white, red, we're all included in the human dilemma, and Darth is scarier this time, because now we learn he's ''human''—he's not a mechanical monster.|Billy Dee Williams|<ref name="Rolling Stone 1980" />}}
Raised in [[Wikipedia:Harlem|Harlem]], [[Wikipedia:New York City|New York City]], Williams performed on Broadway, television, and cinema productions at Hollywood, including 1971's [[Wikipedia:Brian's Song|''Brian's Song'']], 1972's [[Wikipedia:Lady Sings the Blues (film)|''Lady Sings the Blues'']], and 1975's [[Wikipedia:Mahogany (film)|''Mahogany'']]. Williams' friend, author [[Wikipedia:James Baldwin|James Baldwin]], wanted him to play [[Wikipedia:Malcolm X|Malcolm X]] in his screenplay about the American [[Wikipedia:Civil rights movement|civil rights movement]] leader, but it was never produced.<ref name="Guardian">{{WebCite|url=https://www.theguardian.com/culture/2024/feb/21/billy-dee-williams-memoir-star-wars|text=Billy Dee Williams: 'At this stage in my life, I don't need to apologise for anything'|author=David Smith|date=2024-02-21|work=[[Wikipedia:The Guardian|The Guardian]]|archiveurl=https://archive.is/svuOY}}</ref> He had admired the work of a [[Wikipedia:New Hollywood|new wave]] of American film directors such as [[Wikipedia:Francis Ford Coppola|Francis Ford Coppola]], [[George Lucas]], [[Martin Scorsese]], and [[Steven Spielberg]]. He eagerly signed a contract for performing as [[Landonis Balthazar Calrissian|Lando Calrissian]] for two films after hearing the character's name—he said "Calrissian" is an Armenian name—and thought that his cape gave a "bigger than life" aesthetic that he likened to the actor [[Wikipedia:Errol Flynn|Errol Flynn]].<ref name="Guardian" />

[[File:Empire-RollingStones.jpg|thumb|left|280px|Billy Dee Williams and his ''Star Wars'' co-stars on the cover of ''Rolling Stone'']]
Billy Dee Williams was introduced as Lando Calrissian in the [[1980]] film [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back'']]. He was eager to play the first Black ''[[Star Wars]]'' character, having spoken with George Lucas about the antagonist [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]] in the first ''Star Wars'' film, voiced by the prominent Black actor [[James Earl Jones]].<ref name="Rolling Stone 1980" /> Williams said that Lucas had wanted to express a dichotomous symbolism found in the [[Wikipedia:Western film|Western film]] genre where a cowboy dressed in white fights a cowboy dressed in black,<ref name="Guardian" /> but he criticized the impression of a racialist contrast between the "black fear" of Darth Vader contrasted by the "white knight" of the [[Jedi]] [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]], played by veteran actor [[Alec Guinness]]. Williams credited ''Empire'' with detailing the "human dilemma" where Vader is revealed to be a man whose symbolism of darkness is contrasted by the clarity of the protagonists. He had previously discussed Eastern philosophy with ''Empire''{{'s}} director, [[Irvin Kershner]], in particular about the concept of Zen and [[Yoda]], the Jedi teacher introduced in the film, and how ''Star Wars'' should not be about "shoot-'em-up" but realizing that the "body" houses the "better self" that is the "mind."<ref name="Rolling Stone 1980" />

===''Return'' of the saga===
{{Quote|I am Lando Calrissian, and he is me. He is Billy Dee. It's difficult for me to separate myself from that character.|Billy Dee Williams|<ref name="Insider 225 Billy">{{InsiderCite|225|Billy Dee Williams: "What Have We Here?"}}</ref>}}
Billy Dee Williams reprised his role for the [[1983]] film [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']], in which Lando Calrissian helped defeat the [[Galactic Empire]] as a [[Alliance to Restore the Republic|Rebel]] [[general]]. While Williams said he did not rewatch ''Star Wars'' films,<ref name="Guardian" /> the actor also said that he found it "difficult" to separate himself and Lando Calrissian,<ref name="Insider 225 Billy" /> and he imagined his character as a gambler, showman, and entrepeneur akin to [[Wikipedia:Steve Wynn|Steve Wynn]] running [[Wikipedia:Las Vegas|Las Vegas]] rather than just "a general running around shooting things." Williams returned in his role as Lando Calrissian for the final film of the ''Star Wars'' saga, [[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|''Star Wars'': Episode IX ''The Rise of Skywalker'']]. Williams said he enjoyed the filming under the direction of [[J.J. Abrams]], whom he called "the young mogul" in a [[2019]] interview with the [[Wikipedia:Esquire (magazine)|''Esquire'']] magazine in which he fashioned clothes of his own design. He appreciated [[Donald Glover]] playing a younger Lando Calrissian in the film ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]'' released the [[2018|previous year]].<ref name="Esquire">{{WebCite|url=https://www.esquire.com/entertainment/movies/a29817501/billy-dee-williams-star-wars-the-rise-of-skywalker-lando-calrissian-interview|text=The Enduring, Intergalactic Cool of Billy Dee Williams|author=Matt Miller|date=2019-11-26|work=[[Wikipedia:Esquire (magazine)|''Esquire'']]|archivedate=20191126215223}}</ref>

[[File:FalconLeibovitz.jpg|thumb|right|350px|Billy Dee Williams starred in 2019's ''The Rise of Skywalker'']]
After ''The Rise of Skywalker''{{'s}} release in late 2019, when Williams was asked if there was a danger of "too much" ''Star Wars'', he stated: "The only thing that's dangerous is lots of stupid people running around and shooting people." While Williams said that the real world has been dominated by "a European western value system for centuries," he also emphasized that he does not think of himself as Black, just as he does not want to be Black-cast by Hollywood. In contrast to those he described as wanting to "go through the machinations" of politics and "spend most of their time pissed off," Williams stated that his preference is to handle oppression in other ways: "You take a negative and you make it into a positive."<ref name="Guardian" /> He characterized himself as someone who wants to present his points of view in creative ways to contribute "something that you haven't thought about, maybe."<ref name="Esquire" /> A grandfather and prolific painter, Williams had his memoir, ''What Have We Here: Portraits of a Life'', published in [[2024]].<ref name="Guardian" />
